title: Piroshki with cabbage and egg
subtitle: Russian baked buns with cabbage and hard-boiled egg filling
excerpt: Soft yeasted buns filled with braised cabbage, hard-boiled egg, and dill, baked until golden. The classic Russian piroshok, simple enough to make on a weekday, good enough to bring to a picnic.
sourceNotes: Piroshki (singular: pirog or pirozhok) are stuffed pastries or baked buns found across Russian and Eastern European cooking, ranging from large pies (pirogi) to small individual buns (piroshki). The baked version with a yeasted enriched dough is the most common home-cooked form. The cabbage-and-egg filling is one of the most traditional, associated with Russian home baking and the portable food of long train journeys. The filling is cooked dry before it goes into the buns so the moisture does not compromise the dough during baking.

content.text: Piroshki dough is a simple enriched yeasted dough: flour, warm milk, egg, a little sugar and oil. It needs one hour to rise, then is divided into small portions, flattened, filled, and sealed into oval buns. The egg glaze applied before baking gives them their characteristic deep-golden surface. They should be eaten warm from the oven.

The filling must be completely dry, any moisture will make the dough soggy during baking and can prevent the seal from holding. Cook the cabbage until all liquid has evaporated, then cool completely before filling.

items.name: Eggs
items.prepNote: 1 for the dough, 1 beaten for glazing

content.text: For the dough: mix the {{yeast-dried}} and {{granulated-sugar}} into the warm {{whole-milk}} and leave for 5 minutes. Combine with the {{plain-flour}}, 1 egg, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{sunflower-oil}}. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth. Cover and leave to rise for 1 hour until doubled. Meanwhile, cook the {{onion}} in the {{unsalted-butter}} for 5 minutes. Add the {{cabbage-white}} and cook over high heat, stirring, for 12 to 15 minutes until completely soft and dry with no remaining moisture. Cool, then mix with the chopped {{eggs}}, {{dill}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper-ground}}.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Divide the dough into 16 equal pieces. Flatten each into an oval about 10 cm long. Place a tablespoon of filling in the centre, fold the dough over, and pinch firmly to seal, forming an oval bun. Place seam-side down on a lined baking tray with space between each. Beat the remaining egg with a splash of water and brush each bun generously. Leave to rest for 15 minutes. Bake for 18 to 22 minutes until deep golden. Eat warm.

items.fix: The seal must be pressed very firmly and placed face-down on the tray. If the filling is wet, return it to the pan and cook until completely dry
items.cause: Seal not pressed firmly, or filling too wet

items.fix: Use a well-beaten egg (not just white) and apply a generous coat. A second coat applied 5 minutes into baking deepens the colour further
items.cause: Egg wash too thin or applied too sparingly

items.fix: Give the dough the full hour to rise. The buns should bake at 200°C, lower temperatures leave the centre undercooked
items.cause: Under-risen or baked at too high a temperature

content.text: Piroshki are train food in the Russian imagination: sold on station platforms by babushki from large trays covered with cloths, handed through carriage windows on long-distance journeys. They travel well, keep at room temperature for hours, and need no cutlery. The cabbage-and-egg filling is the most traditional and the most portable. At home they are made for Sunday tea, for school lunches, and for any occasion where something substantial and hand-held is needed.
